summary: Fowler explores the concept of 'internal reprogrammability' - the ability to modify software from within its own environment without external toolchains or restarts, as exemplified by Emacs and Smalltalk. He contrasts this with modern plugin architectures that limit customization surface area. While acknowledging challenges like instance fragmentation and upgrade complexity, he argues this capability represents a fundamentally different relationship with tools - one where users craft software to fit their needs rather than accepting fixed products. He questions why this philosophy hasn't been extended more broadly, especially to non-programmers, noting it was central to Alan Kay's dynabook vision.
key_points: Internal reprogrammability means extending software using the same language and environment used to build its core - no separate toolchain or restart required
Modern extensibility through plugins differs fundamentally from true reprogrammability because it limits customization surface area
Fragmentation is the main challenge - personal modifications create custom versions tightly coupled to individual configurations
The Unix command line offers a pale reflection of this philosophy, where custom commands become indistinguishable from built-in ones
Kay's dynabook vision saw users as active programmers of their environment, not passive consumers - a vision largely unrealized
money_quotes: There's a palpable difference between extensions in most of these environments and the kind of reprogramming you do in Emacs or Smalltalk.
Adding a new elisp function is exactly how Emacs is programmed itself - there is no difference between how you program little extensions and the core programming of the software.
Internally reprogrammable tools allow you to add or change any part of your software, allowing you to craft your tools to exactly fit your metaphorical hand.
A reprogrammable system is incredibly powerful. Abusing the power is always possible and it's a point of principle in a reprogrammable system that people *must* be able to abuse it.
For many people the software you use is a relatively fixed product. Even plugins add a relatively limited menu of options.
A large part of why modern dynabooks lack the internal reprogrammability of Kay's vision is because it hasn't been made a high-enough priority.
key_insight: True software extensibility means users program their tools using the same mechanisms that built the core system, creating a fundamentally different and more empowering relationship between people and their software.
